title: Large Scale HEOM Solver Using PETSc - GSoC 2026
text:
Student: Chinmay Tangal Organization: QuTiP - Quantum Toolbox in Python, NumFOCUS
Mentors: Alex Pitchford, Emil VATAI, Neill Lambert
Project Duration: May 2026 - August 2026

title: Large Scale HEOM Solver Using PETSc - GSoC 2026
text:
Project Overview

The Problem
HEOM — the Hierarchical Equations of Motion — is one of the few methods that can exactly simulate open quantum system dynamics without Markov or perturbative approximations. It does this by propagating an entire hierarchy of coupled auxiliary density matrices simultaneously, which is what makes it exact and also what makes it expensive. Memory and compute blow up fast as hierarchy depth or system size grows.
The current QuTiP implementation runs on a single node. The entire hierarchy state lives in one vector on one machine, integrated with a single scipy ODE call. For small systems that’s fine. Push the hierarchy depth past 6, or work with anything beyond a few qubits, and you hit a memory cliff with no workaround — it’s a structural limitation.


What This Project Builds
A distributed execution path for the HEOM solver using PETSc and MPI, so the ODE integration can run across multiple nodes on an HPC cluster. The existing solver stays completely untouched — users who don’t need distributed execution shouldn’t have to think about any of this.


Key Goals

Distributed data layout for the HEOM hierarchy across MPI ranks, designed to keep inter-rank communication local
PETSc TS integration for distributed ODE integration across ranks
Correctness first — the distributed path must produce identical results to the existing solver before any performance work
Benchmarks across hierarchy depths, system sizes, and node counts
HPC documentation for running the solver on real cluster infrastructure

title: Large Scale HEOM Solver Using PETSc - GSoC 2026
text:
Project Timeline

Community Bonding (May 12 – May 24)

Aligned with mentors on tech stack: PETSc and MPI
Set up local development environment with PETSc and MPI
Got familiar with PETSc distributed vectors, matrices, and TS interface



Coding Phase 1 (May 25 – July 5)
Week 1–2: Profile the existing HEOM solver on benchmark systems at different hierarchy depths and system sizes. Document bottlenecks in a reproducible format.
Week 3–4: Implement PETSc-based distributed vectors and matrices for the HEOM hierarchy. Handle distribution of coupled density matrices across MPI ranks.
Week 5–6: Connect distributed data structures to PETSc’s ODE/TS solver. Run small benchmark systems through both solver paths and validate correctness.


Midterm Evaluation (July 6 – July 10)
Deliverables: working distributed HEOM ODE solver via PETSc, correctness validated against existing solver, baseline profiling results documented.


Coding Phase 2 (July 6 – August 16)
Week 7–8: Scale testing at larger hierarchy depths and node counts. Debug MPI-related issues — load imbalance, communication overhead, memory usage across ranks.
Week 9–10: Benchmark distributed solver across different node counts and hierarchy sizes. Optimize remaining bottlenecks.
Week 11–12: Write documentation and HPC tutorials. Finalize test suite. Clean up code from mentor review.


Final Evaluation (August 17 – August 24)
Submit final work product.

title: Large Scale HEOM Solver Using PETSc - GSoC 2026
text:
Implementation

The Design Problem
The coupling structure in HEOM means you can’t naively slice the hierarchy across MPI ranks — adjacent levels need to communicate, so the partition strategy directly determines whether you get a speedup or just move the bottleneck. The key question is whether the data can be laid out so that most communication is between neighboring ranks rather than all-to-all.


Tech Stack

PETSc — distributed vectors, matrices, and ODE integration via the TS interface
MPI / mpi4py — inter-process communication
petsc4py — Python bindings for PETSc
QuTiP — existing HEOM infrastructure, benchmark systems, test suite



What Stays Untouched
The existing HEOMSolver is completely unmodified. The distributed path is additive — users running small systems on a single machine see no changes.

title: Large Scale HEOM Solver Using PETSc - GSoC 2026

Links

title: Week 3: Extracting the RHS Matrix and Building the PETSc Bridge
text:
Week 3 was focused on understanding how the QuTiP HEOM solver actually integrates the equations of motion, and building a path to replace that integration with PETSc.
The profiling from last week showed that most of the runtime is spent inside the ODE integrator. The question this week was: can we extract just the matrix that drives the ODE, and hand it to an external solver without touching anything else in QuTiP?
The answer is yes, and the mechanism is simpler than expected.

title: Week 3: Extracting the RHS Matrix and Building the PETSc Bridge
text:
The Core Idea
HEOM propagates not just the system density matrix \rho_S but an entire hierarchy of auxiliary density operators (ADOs). If you stack all of them into a single vector:

\vec{\rho} = \begin{pmatrix} \text{vec}(\rho^{(\mathbf{0})}) \\ \text{vec}(\rho^{(\mathbf{e}_1)}) \\ \vdots \end{pmatrix}

then all of the coupled HEOM equations collapse into one linear ODE:

\frac{d}{dt}\vec{\rho}(t) = \mathbf{R} \cdot \vec{\rho}(t)

QuTiP builds this matrix \mathbf{R} internally. The method solver.rhs(t) exposes it directly. For a time-independent Hamiltonian, \mathbf{R} does not change, so solver.rhs(0).full() gives you the full matrix as a NumPy array.
This is the only QuTiP-specific step in the whole bridge. Once you have \mathbf{R}, the rest is standard linear algebra.

title: Week 3: Extracting the RHS Matrix and Building the PETSc Bridge
text:
Setup
Standard spin-boson model, same parameters as last week.
import time
import numpy as np
import scipy.sparse as sp
from scipy.integrate import solve_ivp

from qutip import basis, sigmaz, expect
from qutip.solver.heom import HEOMSolver, DrudeLorentzBath

epsilon = 1.0
H = 0.5 * epsilon * sigmaz()
rho0 = basis(2, 0) * basis(2, 0).dag()

lam = 0.1
gamma = 0.5
T = 1.0
Nk = 8
Q = sigmaz()

tlist = np.linspace(0, 20, 500)
depth = 2
n = 2

title: Week 3: Extracting the RHS Matrix and Building the PETSc Bridge
text:
Reference Run
First, run the standard QuTiP solver to get the ground truth.
bath = DrudeLorentzBath(Q=Q, lam=lam, gamma=gamma, T=T, Nk=Nk)
solver = HEOMSolver(H, bath, max_depth=depth, options={"progress_bar": False})

n_ados = solver._n_ados
n_total = n**2 * n_ados

result_qutip = solver.run(rho0, tlist)
sz_qutip = expect(sigmaz(), result_qutip.states)
At depth 2 with Nk=8, this gives 55 ADOs and a state vector of length 220.

title: Week 3: Extracting the RHS Matrix and Building the PETSc Bridge
text:
Extracting the RHS Matrix
RHS = sp.csr_matrix(solver.rhs(0).full())
The matrix is 220×220 with only 598 nonzeros — 98.8% sparse. This is a direct consequence of the hierarchy structure: each ADO only couples to its immediate neighbors one level up or down. The sparsity is what makes distributed execution with PETSc practical, since the inter-rank communication is bounded rather than all-to-all.

title: Week 3: Extracting the RHS Matrix and Building the PETSc Bridge
text:
Building the Initial State Vector
At t = 0, only the physical density matrix is nonzero. All ADOs start at zero because there is no bath memory at the initial time.
rho0_he = np.zeros(n_total, dtype=complex)
rho0_he[:n**2] = rho0.full().ravel("F")
The column-major flattening ("F" order) matches QuTiP’s Liouville-space convention, where \text{vec}(\rho)_{i+nj} = \rho_{ij}.

title: Week 3: Extracting the RHS Matrix and Building the PETSc Bridge
Week 3: Extracting the RHS Matrix and Building the PETSc Bridge
text:
Validation
max_err = np.max(np.abs(sz_qutip - sz_bridge))
print(f"max error vs qutip: {max_err:.2e}")
max error vs qutip: 0.00e+00
The bridge matches QuTiP at machine precision. The two \langle \sigma_z \rangle curves are numerically identical.

title: Week 3: Extracting the RHS Matrix and Building the PETSc Bridge
text:
The PETSc Swap
The entire bridge reduces to one function call:
def rhs_fn(t, y):
    return RHS @ y
Replacing this with PETSc means:

RHS becomes a distributed PETSc.Mat (each MPI rank owns a row slice)
y becomes a distributed PETSc.Vec
RHS @ y becomes A.mult(x, f), which handles inter-rank communication automatically

# petsc equivalent (runs with mpirun -n N python script.py)
def rhs_petsc(ts, t, x, f):
    A.mult(x, f)

ts = PETSc.TS().create(comm=PETSc.COMM_WORLD)
ts.setType(PETSc.TS.Type.RK)
ts.setRHSFunction(rhs_petsc, f)
ts.setTime(tlist[0])
ts.setMaxTime(tlist[-1])
ts.setTimeStep(tlist[1] - tlist[0])
ts.setTolerances(rtol=1e-8, atol=1e-10)
ts.solve(x)
The QuTiP side stays completely untouched. The only thing that changes is what happens after solver.rhs(0).full().

title: Week 3: Extracting the RHS Matrix and Building the PETSc Bridge
text:
Why the Sparsity Matters
At depth 2 the matrix is small enough that this is easy. The motivation becomes clearer looking at larger depths:



Depth
ADOs
State vector
Dense matrix




2
55
220
0.8 MB


3
220
880
12.4 MB


4
715
2860
130.9 MB


5
2002
8008
1,026 MB



At depth 5, storing \mathbf{R} as a dense matrix already requires 1 GB on a single node. In sparse format the same matrix has under 0.1% fill. Distributing those nonzeros across MPI ranks via PETSc’s MatMPIAIJ is the path to running HEOM at depths that are currently out of reach.

title: Week 3: Extracting the RHS Matrix and Building the PETSc Bridge
text:
Next Steps
The next step is getting the PETSc TS call working end-to-end on a single process, then validating that it produces the same result as the SciPy bridge above. After that, the work shifts to the distributed data layout question: which rows of \mathbf{R} live on which MPI rank, and how to minimize inter-rank communication given the hierarchy’s coupling structure.

Full notebook: heom_rhs_bridge.ipynb

title: Week 2: Profiling the Existing HEOM Solver
text:
Coding phase Week 2 was focused on understanding the current HEOM implementation before making any modifications to it.
Before attempting PETSc integration, I wanted to establish a performance baseline for the existing solver, identify the major bottlenecks, and understand how the computational cost scales with hierarchy depth and system size.

title: Week 2: Profiling the Existing HEOM Solver
text:
Benchmarking Hierarchy Depth Scaling
I started with a standard spin-boson model and measured runtime as the hierarchy depth increased. Alongside runtime, I also recorded the number of Auxiliary Density Operators (ADOs), which is one of the main drivers of HEOM complexity.

Benchmark Script
import time
import numpy as np

from qutip import basis, sigmaz
from qutip.solver.heom import HEOMSolver, DrudeLorentzBath

# System parameters
epsilon = 1.0
H = 0.5 * epsilon * sigmaz()

rho0 = basis(2, 0) * basis(2, 0).dag()

# Bath parameters
lam = 0.1
gamma = 0.5
T = 1.0
Nk = 8

Q = sigmaz()

tlist = np.linspace(0, 100, 2000)

depths = [4, 6, 8, 10]

for depth in depths:

    bath = DrudeLorentzBath(
        Q=Q,
        lam=lam,
        gamma=gamma,
        T=T,
        Nk=Nk
    )

    start = time.perf_counter()

    solver = HEOMSolver(
        H,
        bath,
        max_depth=depth,
        options={"progress_bar": False}
    )

    ado_count = len(solver.ados.labels)

    result = solver.run(rho0, tlist)

    runtime = time.perf_counter() - start

    print(
        f"Depth={depth}, "
        f"ADOs={ado_count}, "
        f"Runtime={runtime:.3f} s"
    )


Results



Depth
ADO Count
Runtime (s)




4
715
0.347


6
5005
2.189


8
24310
18.029


10
92378
81.407



The growth is substantial. Increasing the hierarchy depth from 4 to 10 increases the number of ADOs by roughly 129× while runtime increases by approximately 235×.
This immediately highlights why HEOM becomes challenging for larger simulations.

title: Week 2: Profiling the Existing HEOM Solver
text:
Measuring Memory Usage
Runtime is only one part of the picture. Since the long-term goal is distributed-memory execution, I also measured peak memory usage using:
/usr/bin/time -v python benchmark.py
The metric of interest was:
Maximum resident set size (kbytes)

Results



Depth
Peak Memory (MB)




4
121.6


6
133.7


8
199.5


10
458.8



Memory usage increases significantly as the hierarchy expands, particularly at larger depths.

title: Week 2: Profiling the Existing HEOM Solver
text:
Profiling the Solver
After collecting runtime measurements, I used Python’s built-in profiler to determine where execution time was actually being spent.
python -m cProfile -o depth10.prof benchmark.py
The profiling data was inspected using:
python -m pstats depth10.prof
and sorted by cumulative runtime:
sort cumulative
stats 30

Dominant Bottlenecks
The largest contributors to runtime were:



Function
Description




scipy.integrate._vode.zvode
ODE integration


_calculate_rhs
HEOM RHS construction


_rhs
RHS evaluation


_restore_state
State reconstruction


_grad_prev / _grad_next
Hierarchy traversal



The profile clearly showed that most of the runtime is spent either integrating the large HEOM system of equations or constructing and applying the hierarchy coupling structure.
These are likely to be key targets for future optimization and parallelization.

title: Week 2: Profiling the Existing HEOM Solver
text:
Scaling with System Size
Hierarchy depth is only one source of complexity. The cost of operations also depends on the size of the underlying quantum system.
To investigate this, I repeated the benchmark using a two-qubit Hamiltonian.

Two-Qubit Benchmark
import time
import numpy as np

from qutip import (
    basis,
    sigmaz,
    qeye,
    tensor
)

from qutip.solver.heom import HEOMSolver, DrudeLorentzBath

H = (
    0.5 * tensor(sigmaz(), qeye(2))
    + 0.5 * tensor(qeye(2), sigmaz())
)

psi0 = tensor(basis(2, 0), basis(2, 0))
rho0 = psi0 * psi0.dag()

lam = 0.1
gamma = 0.5
T = 1.0
Nk = 8

Q = tensor(sigmaz(), qeye(2))

tlist = np.linspace(0, 100, 2000)

depths = [4, 6, 8, 10]

for depth in depths:

    bath = DrudeLorentzBath(
        Q=Q,
        lam=lam,
        gamma=gamma,
        T=T,
        Nk=Nk
    )

    start = time.perf_counter()

    solver = HEOMSolver(
        H,
        bath,
        max_depth=depth,
        options={"progress_bar": False}
    )

    ado_count = len(solver.ados.labels)

    result = solver.run(rho0, tlist)

    runtime = time.perf_counter() - start

    print(
        f"Depth={depth}, "
        f"ADOs={ado_count}, "
        f"Runtime={runtime:.3f} s"
    )


Results



Depth
ADO Count
Runtime (s)




4
715
0.861


6
5005
11.169


8
24310
68.927


10
92378
308.473



The number of ADOs remains unchanged because the hierarchy structure is the same, but each ADO becomes more expensive to evolve due to the larger Hilbert space dimension.
This demonstrates a second scaling challenge:

Increasing hierarchy depth increases the number of ADOs.
Increasing system size increases the computational cost per ADO.

Both effects contribute significantly to runtime growth.

title: Week 2: Profiling the Existing HEOM Solver
text:
Key Findings
By the end of the week I had established a baseline performance profile of the current HEOM implementation.

Runtime grows rapidly with hierarchy depth.
Memory usage increases substantially as the hierarchy expands.
ODE integration dominates overall runtime.
RHS construction and hierarchy traversal are major computational bottlenecks.
Larger quantum systems significantly increase the cost of evolving each ADO.

Most importantly, these measurements provide a reproducible baseline against which future PETSc-based implementations can be compared.

title: Week 2: Profiling the Existing HEOM Solver
text:
Looking Ahead
The next step is to dig deeper into the HEOM source code and understand how hierarchy data structures, ADO mappings, and RHS assembly are implemented internally.
The profiling results point directly toward the parts of the codebase that deserve the closest attention:

_calculate_rhs
_rhs
_grad_prev
_grad_next
_restore_state

Understanding how these pieces interact will be important before attempting any distributed-memory redesign using PETSc vectors, matrices, and TS integrators.

Next up: exploring the HEOM internals and identifying how the hierarchy structure can be mapped onto PETSc’s distributed data structures.

title: Exciting News: I’m Joining Google Summer of Code 2026!
text:
A few months ago, I put together a GSoC proposal half-convinced it wouldn’t go anywhere. I’d been contributing to QuTiP for a while, understood the codebase well enough to spot a real problem, and figured — why not try?
I’m happy to share that I’ve been selected as a Google Summer of Code 2026 contributor! 🚀

title: Exciting News: I’m Joining Google Summer of Code 2026!
text:
What I’ll Be Working On
Here’s the problem: the HEOM solver in QuTiP is single-node. The entire hierarchy state lives in one vector on one machine, integrated with a single scipy ODE call. For small systems that’s fine. But push the hierarchy depth past 6, or work with anything beyond a few qubits, and you hit a memory cliff fast. There’s no workaround — it’s a structural limitation.
My project builds the distributed path that’s been missing: integrating PETSc and MPI into the solver so the ODE integration can run across multiple nodes on an HPC cluster.
The project will include:

Distributed data layout for the HEOM hierarchy across MPI ranks, designed to keep inter-rank communication local rather than all-to-all
PETSc TS integration for distributed vector and matrix operations and time integration
Correctness validation against the existing solver on standard benchmark systems
Performance benchmarks across hierarchy depths, system sizes, and node counts
HPC documentation and tutorials for running the solver on real cluster infrastructure

The existing solver stays completely untouched — if you don’t need distributed execution, nothing changes for you.

title: Exciting News: I’m Joining Google Summer of Code 2026!
text:
Looking Forward
I’ve spent the past few months going through the QuTiP codebase, submitting PRs, and getting a feel for how the project works. That groundwork made writing the proposal a lot more concrete — I wasn’t guessing at the architecture, I’d actually traced through it.
Now I get to spend the summer working on the part that genuinely interested me from the start: the design problem of distributing a coupled hierarchy without turning communication into the new bottleneck.
Over the next few months, I’ll be:

Profiling the existing solver and nailing down the distributed data layout with mentors
Building and testing the PETSc-based execution path
Validating correctness and running scaling benchmarks on HPC infrastructure
Documenting everything here as I go

Expect posts on the design decisions, the tricky bits, and probably a few dead ends along the way.

title: Exciting News: I’m Joining Google Summer of Code 2026!
text:
Acknowledgments
Thanks to the QuTiP team — especially my mentors Alex Pitchford and Eric Giguère — for taking on this project and for the feedback they gave during the proposal stage. Looking forward to the summer.

Following along, working on something related, or also doing GSoC this year? Feel free to reach out — always happy to talk HPC, open quantum systems, or open source.






TipInterested in QuTiP?



Check out the QuTiP documentation to learn more about quantum simulations in Python!


Tags: GSoC2026, HPC, OpenQuantumSystems, OpenSource, Python, QuTiP, NumFOCUS, PETSc, MPI

title: Welcome to My GSoC 2026 Journey
text:
Hi, I’m Chinmay Tangal – a Computer Engineering undergrad with a growing interest in high-performance computing, open quantum systems, and open-source scientific software. I’m excited to be part of Google Summer of Code 2026 with NumFOCUS, working on a project that sits right at the intersection of quantum physics and systems-level computing.
This blog is where I’ll document my GSoC experience, the technical problems I run into, and how I’m thinking through building a cluster-ready quantum solver.









I’m building a distributed HEOM solver for QuTiP (Quantum Toolbox in Python). HEOM — the Hierarchical Equations of Motion — is one of the few methods that can exactly simulate open quantum system dynamics without Markov or perturbative approximations. The catch is that it’s expensive: memory and compute blow up fast as hierarchy depth or system size grows. The current QuTiP implementation runs on a single node, which puts a hard ceiling on what’s realistically solvable.
What I’m Building:

A distributed execution path for the HEOM solver using PETSc and MPI, so the ODE integration can run across multiple nodes on an HPC cluster
A careful distributed data layout for the hierarchy that keeps inter-rank communication manageable
Correctness validation against the existing solver on standard benchmark systems
Performance benchmarks across hierarchy depths, system sizes, and node counts
Documentation and tutorials for running the solver on real HPC infrastructure

Technologies & Tools:

Primary: Python, PETSc, MPI, QuTiP, SciPy
Development: Git, GitHub, Jupyter Notebooks
Documentation: LaTeX, Matplotlib
Testing: pytest framework

Mentors: Alex Pitchford, Emil VATAI, Neill Lambert
The existing solver stays completely untouched — users who don’t need distributed execution shouldn’t have to think about any of this.
🔗 View Project Details on GSoC Website






May 2026
I’d been contributing to QuTiP for a few months when I decided to put together a GSoC proposal. I didn’t expect it to go anywhere — but here we are…
Read more →

Python and C programming

Technical tutorials and HPC workflows

title: Google Summer of Code 2026 | QuTiP | NumFOCUS
text: I’m a computer engineering student who stumbled into open source through quantum physics — and honestly never looked back. This summer I’m a Google Summer of Code 2026 contributor, working on making QuTiP’s HEOM solver scale beyond a single machine.

title: Google Summer of Code 2026 | QuTiP | NumFOCUS
text: I’m a computer engineering student who stumbled into open source through quantum physics — and honestly never looked back. This summer I’m a Google Summer of Code 2026 contributor, working on making QuTiP’s HEOM solver scale beyond a single machine.

title: Google Summer of Code 2026 | QuTiP | NumFOCUS
text:
Current Work
Google Summer of Code 2026 Contributor
May 2026 - Present
Working with NumFOCUS on building a distributed HEOM solver for QuTiP (Quantum Toolbox in Python). The project integrates PETSc and MPI into the existing solver so that large-scale open quantum system simulations can be distributed across HPC clusters — without touching anything for users who don’t need it.
Key contributions: - Profiling the existing HEOM solver to identify memory and compute bottlenecks - Designing a distributed data layout for the hierarchy across MPI ranks - Integrating PETSc’s TS interface for distributed ODE integration - Writing HPC tutorials and documentation for real cluster workflows

title: Community Bonding Period: Setting the Foundation for Success
text: The community bonding period (May 12 - May 24) is wrapped up. Honestly it went by faster than I expected.

title: Community Bonding Period: Setting the Foundation for Success
text:
Meeting the Team
Had my first meeting with Alex, Emil, Neill and Simon early in the period. Main thing we sorted out was the tech stack — we decided to go with PETSc and MPI for the distributed solver. PETSc handles the distributed data structures and ODE integration through its TS interface, MPI handles the inter-process communication. Makes sense given that PETSc is basically the standard for this kind of scientific computing work on HPC clusters.
Once that was settled, the rest of bonding was mostly about getting everything running on my machine and getting comfortable with it.

title: Community Bonding Period: Setting the Foundation for Success
text:
Getting the Stack Running
Setting up PETSc locally was… an experience. It’s not the kind of library you just pip install and move on. Got through it though — PETSc built, MPI configured, and a basic test confirming distributed vectors were actually distributing across processes the way they should.
Spent the remaining time going through PETSc’s documentation properly, particularly the TS interface which is what we’ll be using for the time integration. Also ran through some MPI basics to make sure I’m solid on communication patterns before I need to think about them in the context of the HEOM hierarchy.

title: Community Bonding Period: Setting the Foundation for Success
text:
Looking Ahead
Stack is set up, I know what I’m working with. Coding phase starts now — first order of business is profiling the existing solver to understand where it actually breaks down before building anything new.

Next up: Profiling the existing HEOM solver.

title: Week 1: Getting Hands-On with MPI and PETSc
text: Coding phase has started. Week 1 was less about writing solver code and more about actually getting comfortable with the tools I’ll be using — MPI and PETSc. Worked through a set of exercises, each one building on the last.

title: Week 1: Getting Hands-On with MPI and PETSc
text:
Starting with MPI
Started with the basics — splitting work across processes, each rank handles its chunk, gather results back to rank 0.
from mpi4py import MPI

comm = MPI.COMM_WORLD 
rank = comm.Get_rank()
size = comm.Get_size()

chunk = 100 // size  
start = rank * chunk + 1   
end   = start + chunk

local_results = [(n, n * n) for n in range(start, end)] 

print(f"Rank {rank}: computed {start}² to {end-1}²")

all_results = comm.gather(local_results, root=0)

if rank == 0:
    flat = [item for sublist in all_results for item in sublist]
    print(f"\nRank 0 has all {len(flat)} results")
    for n, sq in flat[:5]:
        print(f"  {n}² = {sq}")
    print("  ...")
Simple, but it made the mental model concrete.

title: Week 1: Getting Hands-On with MPI and PETSc
text:
PETSc Distributed Vectors
Next step was PETSc. Created a distributed vector, filled it with values, verified each rank owned the right slice.
import sys
from mpi4py import MPI
import petsc4py
petsc4py.init(sys.argv)
from petsc4py import PETSc

comm = MPI.COMM_WORLD
rank = comm.Get_rank()

x = PETSc.Vec().create(comm=PETSc.COMM_WORLD)
x.setSizes(100)
x.setFromOptions()
x.setUp()

lo, hi = x.getOwnershipRange()

for i in range(lo, hi):
    x.setValue(i, (i + 1) ** 2)

x.assemblyBegin()
x.assemblyEnd()

vals = x.getValues(range(lo, hi))
print(f"Rank {rank} owns indices {lo}–{hi-1}, first={int(vals[0])}, last={int(vals[-1])}")

total = x.sum()
if rank == 0:
    print(f"\nSum of all squares = {int(total)}")
    print(f"Expected           = {sum(i*i for i in range(1,101))}")

x.destroy()
getOwnershipRange() is the pattern I’ll be using constantly — each rank knows which slice of the global data it owns.

title: Week 1: Getting Hands-On with MPI and PETSc
text:
Distributed Matrix-Vector Multiplication
Built a tridiagonal matrix distributed across ranks and multiplied it by a distributed vector. This is roughly the shape of what the HEOM RHS computation will look like.
import sys
from mpi4py import MPI
import petsc4py
petsc4py.init(sys.argv)
from petsc4py import PETSc

comm = MPI.COMM_WORLD
rank = comm.Get_rank()
size = comm.Get_size()

global_size = 100

x = PETSc.Vec().create(comm=comm)
x.setSizes(global_size)
x.setFromOptions()
x.setUp()

v_lo, v_hi = x.getOwnershipRange()
for i in range(v_lo, v_hi):
    x.setValue(i, float(i))
x.assemblyBegin()
x.assemblyEnd()

A = PETSc.Mat().create(comm=comm)
A.setSizes([global_size, global_size])
A.setType('aij')
A.setFromOptions()
A.setUp()

m_lo, m_hi = A.getOwnershipRange()
for i in range(m_lo, m_hi):
    if i > 0:
        A.setValue(i, i - 1, -1.0)
    A.setValue(i, i, 2.0)
    if i < global_size - 1:
        A.setValue(i, i + 1, -1.0)

A.assemblyBegin()
A.assemblyEnd()

y = PETSc.Vec().create(comm=comm)
y.setSizes(global_size)
y.setFromOptions()
y.setUp()

A.mult(x, y)

y_lo, y_hi = y.getOwnershipRange()
local_vals = y.getValues(range(y_lo, y_hi))
print(f"Rank {rank} checked indices {y_lo} to {y_hi-1}.")
print(f"  -> Index {y_lo} value: {local_vals[0]}")
print(f"  -> Index {y_hi-1} value: {local_vals[-1]}")

x.destroy()
y.destroy()
A.destroy()
The assembly step is where MPI communication happens across rank boundaries — PETSc handles that internally.

title: Week 1: Getting Hands-On with MPI and PETSc
text:
Multi-Index Mapping (The HEOM Layout)
This one was directly relevant to the project. In HEOM, auxiliary density matrices are labeled by multi-indices — (0,0), (1,0), (0,1), etc. PETSc only understands flat linear indices, so I needed a mapping function that converts hierarchy coordinates into a 1D index.
import sys
from mpi4py import MPI
import petsc4py
petsc4py.init(sys.argv)
from petsc4py import PETSc

comm = MPI.COMM_WORLD
rank = comm.Get_rank()

GRID_DIM = 5
global_size = GRID_DIM * GRID_DIM

def get_flat_index(n1, n2):
    return n1 * GRID_DIM + n2

x = PETSc.Vec().create(comm=comm)
x.setSizes(global_size)
x.setFromOptions()
x.setUp()

lo, hi = x.getOwnershipRange()

for n1 in range(GRID_DIM):
    for n2 in range(GRID_DIM):
        flat_idx = get_flat_index(n1, n2)
        if lo <= flat_idx < hi:
            physics_value = float(n1 * 10 + n2)
            x.setValue(flat_idx, physics_value)

x.assemblyBegin()
x.assemblyEnd()

target_idx = get_flat_index(3, 2)
if lo <= target_idx < hi:
    val = x.getValues([target_idx])[0]
    print(f"Rank {rank} owns flat index {target_idx} (grid (3,2)). Value: {val}")

x.destroy()
This mapping is going to be central to the actual implementation.

title: Week 1: Getting Hands-On with MPI and PETSc
text:
ODE Integration with PETSc TS
Last exercise — hooking up PETSc’s time-stepper (TS) to actually solve an ODE in parallel. Used a simple decay system dx/dt = -0.5x so I could verify the output analytically.
import sys
from mpi4py import MPI
import petsc4py
petsc4py.init(sys.argv)
from petsc4py import PETSc

comm = MPI.COMM_WORLD
rank = comm.Get_rank()
global_size = 10

A = PETSc.Mat().create(comm=comm)
A.setSizes([global_size, global_size])
A.setType('aij')
A.setUp()

m_lo, m_hi = A.getOwnershipRange()
for i in range(m_lo, m_hi):
    A.setValue(i, i, -0.5)
A.assemblyBegin()
A.assemblyEnd()

x = PETSc.Vec().create(comm=comm)
x.setSizes(global_size)
x.setUp()
v_lo, v_hi = x.getOwnershipRange()
for i in range(v_lo, v_hi):
    x.setValue(i, 10.0)
x.assemblyBegin()
x.assemblyEnd()

def rhs_function(ts, t, vec_x, vec_f):
    A.mult(vec_x, vec_f)

ts = PETSc.TS().create(comm=comm)
ts.setProblemType(ts.ProblemType.LINEAR)
ts.setRHSFunction(rhs_function)
ts.setType('rk')
ts.setTime(0.0)
ts.setMaxTime(2.0)
ts.setTimeStep(0.1)
ts.setFromOptions()

ts.solve(x)

v_lo, v_hi = x.getOwnershipRange()
local_vals = x.getValues(range(v_lo, v_hi))
if rank == 0:
    print(f"Initial value was 10.0. After evolving to t=2.0, index 0 value is: {local_vals[0]:.4f}")
    # Analytical: 10 * e^(-0.5 * 2) ≈ 3.6788

x.destroy()
A.destroy()
ts.destroy()
This is the pattern the distributed HEOM solver will follow — define a distributed RHS function, hand it to PETSc TS, let it integrate. Good to have a working toy version before dealing with the actual hierarchy.

Next up: Profiling the existing HEOM solver to understand where it breaks down.

title: Google Summer of Code 2026 | QuTiP | NumFOCUS
text:
import time
import numpy as np
import scipy.sparse as sp
from scipy.integrate import solve_ivp
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

from qutip import basis, sigmaz, expect
from qutip.solver.heom import HEOMSolver, DrudeLorentzBath


# system
epsilon = 1.0
H = 0.5 * epsilon * sigmaz()
rho0 = basis(2, 0) * basis(2, 0).dag()

# bath
lam = 0.1
gamma = 0.5
T = 1.0
Nk = 8
Q = sigmaz()

tlist = np.linspace(0, 20, 500)
depth = 2
n = 2


# reference run using the standard qutip solver
bath = DrudeLorentzBath(Q=Q, lam=lam, gamma=gamma, T=T, Nk=Nk)
solver = HEOMSolver(H, bath, max_depth=depth, options={"progress_bar": False})

n_ados = solver._n_ados
n_total = n**2 * n_ados

print(f"ADOs: {n_ados}")
print(f"state vector length: {n_total}")

t0 = time.perf_counter()
result_qutip = solver.run(rho0, tlist)
t_qutip = time.perf_counter() - t0

print(f"qutip runtime: {t_qutip:.3f} s")

sz_qutip = expect(sigmaz(), result_qutip.states)


# extract the RHS matrix
# solver.rhs(0) returns the full HEOM RHS as a qutip Qobj
# .full() converts it to a numpy array
# for a time-independent system this matrix does not change, so rhs(0) is enough
RHS = sp.csr_matrix(solver.rhs(0).full())

print(f"RHS shape: {RHS.shape}")
print(f"non-zeros: {RHS.nnz} out of {n_total**2} ({100*RHS.nnz/n_total**2:.1f}%)")


# build the extended initial state vector
# HEOM state = [vec(rho_s), 0, 0, ...] where all ADOs start at zero
# vec uses column-major order (qutip's convention)
rho0_he = np.zeros(n_total, dtype=complex)
rho0_he[:n**2] = rho0.full().ravel("F")

print(f"first 4 entries: {rho0_he[:4].real}  (should be [1, 0, 0, 0])")


# solve the same ODE using an external integrator
# this is the part that will be replaced by petsc later
# the ODE is: d/dt rho_he = RHS @ rho_he

def rhs_fn(t, y):
    return RHS @ y

t0 = time.perf_counter()
sol = solve_ivp(
    rhs_fn,
    t_span=(tlist[0], tlist[-1]),
    y0=rho0_he,
    t_eval=tlist,
    method="RK45",
    rtol=1e-8,
    atol=1e-10,
)
t_bridge = time.perf_counter() - t0

print(f"bridge runtime: {t_bridge:.3f} s")
print(f"success: {sol.success}")


# extract rho_s(t) from the bridge solution
# first n^2 entries of each column are vec(rho_s), reshape back to n x n

def extract_rho_s(y):
    return y[:n**2].reshape((n, n), order="F")

sz_bridge = np.array([
    extract_rho_s(sol.y[:, i])[0, 0].real - extract_rho_s(sol.y[:, i])[1, 1].real
    for i in range(len(tlist))
])


# validation
max_err = np.max(np.abs(sz_qutip - sz_bridge))
print(f"max error vs qutip: {max_err:.2e}")

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(12, 4))

axes[0].plot(tlist, sz_qutip, lw=2, label="QuTiP")
axes[0].plot(tlist, sz_bridge, lw=1.5, ls="--", label="bridge (scipy RK45)")
axes[0].set_xlabel("time")
axes[0].set_ylabel(r"$\langle \sigma_z \rangle$")
axes[0].legend()
axes[0].grid(alpha=0.3)

axes[1].semilogy(tlist, np.abs(sz_qutip - sz_bridge) + 1e-16)
axes[1].set_xlabel("time")
axes[1].set_ylabel("pointwise error")
axes[1].grid(alpha=0.3)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig("bridge_validation.png", dpi=150, bbox_inches="tight")
plt.show()


# petsc bridge
# swap USE_PETSC to True after installing petsc4py
# install: conda install -c conda-forge petsc petsc4py mpi4py

USE_PETSC = False

if USE_PETSC:
    import petsc4py
    petsc4py.init()
    from petsc4py import PETSc

    # build petsc matrix from CSR data
    A = PETSc.Mat().createAIJ(
        size=(n_total, n_total),
        csr=(RHS.indptr, RHS.indices, RHS.data),
        comm=PETSc.COMM_WORLD,
    )
    A.assemblyBegin()
    A.assemblyEnd()

    x = A.createVecRight()
    f = A.createVecLeft()
    x.setValues(range(n_total), rho0_he)
    x.assemblyBegin()
    x.assemblyEnd()

    def rhs_petsc(ts, t, x, f):
        A.mult(x, f)

    ts = PETSc.TS().create(comm=PETSc.COMM_WORLD)
    ts.setType(PETSc.TS.Type.RK)
    ts.setRHSFunction(rhs_petsc, f)
    ts.setTime(tlist[0])
    ts.setMaxTime(tlist[-1])
    ts.setTimeStep(tlist[1] - tlist[0])
    ts.setTolerances(rtol=1e-8, atol=1e-10)
    ts.setFromOptions()

    t0 = time.perf_counter()
    ts.solve(x)
    t_petsc = time.perf_counter() - t0

    y_final = np.array(x.getArray())
    rho_final = extract_rho_s(y_final)
    sz_final = rho_final[0, 0].real - rho_final[1, 1].real

    print(f"petsc runtime: {t_petsc:.3f} s")
    print(f"<sigma_z> at t_final: {sz_final:.6f}")
    print(f"qutip reference:      {sz_qutip[-1]:.6f}")
else:
    print("petsc bridge skipped. set USE_PETSC = True to run.")

title: Week 4: Building the HEOM RHS from Scratch and Going Fully Distributed
text: Week 4 was the most technically involved week so far. The goal shifted from using QuTiP’s HEOM solver to understanding it well enough to replace its internals — rebuilding the RHS matrix from first principles, assembling it directly into PETSc, and finally distributing that assembly across MPI ranks so that the matrix never has to exist in full on any single machine.

title: Week 4: Building the HEOM RHS from Scratch and Going Fully Distributed
text:
Recap: Where We Left Off
By the end of Week 3, the pipeline looked like this:

QuTiP builds R → extract with solver.rhs(0).full() → hand to PETSc TS → solve

That worked and gave a 16× speedup over QuTiP at small scale. But the critical bottleneck was still there: QuTiP assembles the entire R matrix on a single process before we even touch PETSc. For large hierarchy depths, this single-process assembly is what kills scalability — not the ODE solve.
This week’s work addresses that bottleneck directly.

title: Week 4: Building the HEOM RHS from Scratch and Going Fully Distributed
text:
The Roadmap
The approach this week followed two distinct tracks, called Optimal 1 and Optimal 2 in the diagram below.



Pipeline overview showing all tracks from baseline to fully distributed


Optimal 1 (Hybrid): Build R ourselves using QuTiP’s internal dictionary approach, assemble into PETSc on a single process, solve with PETSc TS + MPI. The assembly is still serial, but the construction logic is now fully transparent and under our control — no longer a black box.
Optimal 2 (Fully distributed): Each MPI rank builds only the rows of R that it owns. The matrix is never assembled on a single machine. Assembly and solve are both distributed from the start.

title: Week 4: Building the HEOM RHS from Scratch and Going Fully Distributed
text:
Understanding the HEOM Structure
Before writing any code, it is worth being precise about what R actually contains, because this is what the from-scratch construction must reproduce.
The full HEOM state vector is:

\vec{\rho} = \begin{pmatrix}
\text{vec}(\rho^{(\mathbf{0})}) \\
\text{vec}(\rho^{(\mathbf{e}_1)}) \\
\text{vec}(\rho^{(\mathbf{e}_2)}) \\
\vdots
\end{pmatrix}

where \rho^{(\mathbf{n})} is the ADO with multi-index \mathbf{n} = (n_1, n_2, \ldots, n_K) and n_k counts how many times the k-th bath exponential mode has been “excited.” The matrix R is block structured: each block corresponds to a pair of ADOs, and the blocks are sparse because each ADO only couples to its immediate neighbours (one tier up or down, per Matsubara mode).
Concretely, for each ADO at multi-index \mathbf{n}:
Diagonal block — system Liouvillian plus ADO decay: 
\mathcal{L}_{\text{sys}} - \left(\sum_k n_k \nu_k\right) \mathbf{I}

where \mathcal{L}_{\text{sys}} = -i[H, \cdot] is the system Liouvillian in superoperator form, and \nu_k are the bath exponent frequencies (Matsubara rates).
Coupling down (to ADO \mathbf{n} - \mathbf{e}_k, one tier below): 
-i \cdot n_k \cdot \left[c_k \left(Q \rho - \rho Q^\dagger \right)\right]

with c_k the bath correlation coefficient for mode k. For RI-type exponents (which arise in the Drude-Lorentz bath), there is an additional term involving the imaginary part coefficient c_k^{(2)}: 
n_k \left[-i c_k (\mathcal{L}Q - \mathcal{R}Q) + c_k^{(2)} (\mathcal{L}Q + \mathcal{R}Q)\right]
 where \mathcal{L}Q \cdot = Q\rho and \mathcal{R}Q \cdot = \rho Q are the left- and right-multiplication superoperators.
Coupling up (to ADO \mathbf{n} + \mathbf{e}_k, one tier above): 
-i \left(Q\rho - \rho Q\right)

Notice that the coupling-up term does not depend on c_k. This is a key structural feature of the HEOM — the bath coefficients appear only in the downward coupling.

The ADO Index Dictionary
QuTiP v5 organises all of this via a HierarchyADOs object attached to the solver as solver.ados. The critical attributes are:
ados.labels      # list of ADO multi-index tuples, e.g. [(0,0,0), (1,0,0), ...]
ados.idx(label)  # integer position of a label in the big matrix
ados.next(label, k)  # label one step UP in mode k (None if at max depth)
ados.prev(label, k)  # label one step DOWN in mode k (None if at zero)
ados.vk          # list of nu_k values
ados.ck          # list of c_k values
ados.ck2         # list of c_k^(2) values (None for pure-R exponents)
ados.exponents   # list of BathExponent objects with .type, .ck, .vk, .ck2, .Q
The next and prev methods encode the hierarchy connectivity. When you call ados.next(label, k), you get the label of the ADO that is one level higher in mode k, or None if the depth ceiling is reached. This is exactly what you need to know which (row, column) block pairs to fill in R.

title: Week 4: Building the HEOM RHS from Scratch and Going Fully Distributed
section: Optimal 1: Building R with the Dictionary Approach
text:
Optimal 1: Building R with the Dictionary Approach
The construction loop in heom_optimal1.py mirrors QuTiP’s internal _rhs() method exactly, but using scipy sparse matrices instead of QuTiP’s internal data types:
rows, cols, data = [], [], []

def add_block(i_block, j_block, mat):
    r0, c0 = i_block * Nsup, j_block * Nsup
    coo = sp.csr_matrix(mat).tocoo()
    for r, c, v in zip(coo.row, coo.col, coo.data):
        rows.append(r0 + r)
        cols.append(c0 + c)
        data.append(v)

for idx, label in enumerate(ado_labels):
    label = list(label)

    # Diagonal: system Liouvillian + ADO decay
    decay = sum(label[k] * exponents[k].vk for k in range(len(exponents)))
    add_block(idx, idx, L_sys - decay * sp.eye(Nsup, format="csr"))

    for k, ex in enumerate(exponents):
        nk = label[k]

        # Coupling DOWN (to tier below)
        if nk >= 1:
            lbl_down = tuple(label[j] - int(j == k) for j in range(len(exponents)))
            if lbl_down in label_to_idx:
                add_block(idx, label_to_idx[lbl_down], -1j * nk * comm_Q)

        # Coupling UP (to tier above)
        if sum(label) < depth:
            lbl_up = tuple(label[j] + int(j == k) for j in range(len(exponents)))
            if lbl_up in label_to_idx:
                add_block(idx, label_to_idx[lbl_up], -1j * (ck * Q_l - np.conj(ck) * Q_r))

RHS_opt1 = sp.csr_matrix((data, (rows, cols)), shape=(n_total, n_total), dtype=complex)

Output and a Subtle Bug
ADOs: 55   state-vector length: 220
RHS shape: (220, 220)   nnz: 578
max |RHS_opt1 - RHS_qutip| = 3.98e+00
max |err| vs QuTiP ref    : 0.00e+00
There is an interesting tension in these two lines. The RHS matrix differs from QuTiP’s by up to 3.98 in maximum entry, yet the solution trajectory is exact to machine precision. This is not a coincidence — it reveals something important about the coupling-up formula.
The correct coupling-up operator is: 
-i(Q\rho - \rho Q)
 which does not involve c_k at all. The code above used -i(c_k Q\rho - c_k^* \rho Q) instead, which is wrong for modes where c_k \neq 1. However, for the specific initial state \rho_0 = |0\rangle\langle 0| (which is diagonal), the coupling-up operator maps the initial state to zero:

(Q\rho_0 - \rho_0 Q)_{ij} = (\sigma_z)_{ii}\rho_{ij} - \rho_{ij}(\sigma_z)_{jj}

Since \rho_0 is diagonal, the off-diagonal elements are zero, and only the diagonal blocks of Q\rho - \rho Q are nonzero. For \sigma_z as the coupling operator, these diagonal blocks are also zero. So both the correct and incorrect coupling-up formulas give the same result for this specific initial condition.
The fix is to use -i \cdot \text{comm}_Q (independent of c_k) for the coupling-up term, which is what Optimal 2 and QuTiP’s _grad_next_bosonic both do. The Optimal 1 result is still correct for this test case, but would give wrong answers for a non-diagonal initial state.


Runtimes (Optimal 1, depth=2, 4 MPI ranks)



Method
Runtime




QuTiP baseline
0.048 s


SciPy RK45 (our RHS)
0.021 s


PETSc TS + 4 MPI ranks
0.003 s



The PETSc solve is 16× faster than QuTiP and 7× faster than scipy on this small problem. At this scale the problem is too small to show the real benefit — the point is that the same code will scale to much larger problems without modification.

title: Week 4: Building the HEOM RHS from Scratch and Going Fully Distributed
text:
Optimal 2: Fully Distributed Assembly
In Optimal 1, the construction of RHS_opt1 still happens on a single process before being loaded into PETSc. For large hierarchy depths this assembly becomes the bottleneck.
Optimal 2 eliminates this bottleneck. Each MPI rank asks PETSc what rows it owns (A.getOwnershipRange()), then builds only those rows locally — the full matrix never exists on any single machine.
A = PETSc.Mat().createAIJ(size=(n_total, n_total), comm=comm_petsc)
A.setUp()
rstart, rend = A.getOwnershipRange()

for ado_idx, label in enumerate(ado_labels):
    row_off = ado_idx * Nsup
    # ... compute diagonal and off-diagonal blocks ...
    for local_r in range(Nsup):
        gr = row_off + local_r
        if rstart <= gr < rend:   # only fill rows this rank owns
            A.setValue(gr, gc, gv)
This is the key structural change: the if rstart <= gr < rend guard means each rank performs exactly its share of the work, with no redundant computation and no global gather of the matrix.

Output (depth=8, Nk=8, 4 MPI ranks)
K = 9 exponential terms
ADOs: 24,310   total DOF: 97,240

Rank 0: rows 0–24309      (177,613 nonzeros)
Rank 1: rows 24310–48619  (172,145 nonzeros)
Rank 2: rows 48620–72929  ( 77,087 nonzeros)
Rank 3: rows 72930–97239  ( 82,233 nonzeros)

PETSc+MPI distributed runtime: 0.088 s
<sz> at t_end: 1.000000
Total nonzeros across all ranks: 509,078 in a 97,240×97,240 matrix. That is a fill fraction of 0.0054% — the matrix is extremely sparse.
The dense equivalent would require: 
97{,}240^2 \times 16 \text{ bytes} \approx 151 \text{ GB}
 which would be impossible to store on a single machine, let alone solve. The sparse distributed approach stores only the 509,078 nonzeros, split evenly across ranks.


Load Balance
One thing the output reveals is a load imbalance across ranks:



Rank
Rows
Nonzeros
Avg nnz/row




0
24,310
177,613
7.3


1
24,310
172,145
7.1


2
24,310
77,087
3.2


3
24,310
82,233
3.4



Ranks 0 and 1 own roughly twice the work of ranks 2 and 3. This is because PETSc assigns rows by contiguous index, and the lower-tier ADOs (small \sum n_k) are indexed first. Lower-tier ADOs have more neighbours — both upward and downward coupling terms — so their rows are denser. The upper-tier ADOs (high \sum n_k, near the truncation boundary) have fewer neighbours because some upward couplings are cut off by the depth ceiling.
This is a known issue with naive row-based partitioning of hierarchy matrices. A better partitioning would assign rows by tier level to equalise work per rank. That is a future optimisation.

title: Week 4: Building the HEOM RHS from Scratch and Going Fully Distributed
text:
Scaling: Why This Matters
The table below shows why distributed assembly is not optional for large depths:



Depth
ADOs
DOF
Dense matrix




2
55
220
0.8 MB


4
715
2,860
130.9 MB


6
5,005
20,020
6.4 GB


8
24,310
97,240
151 GB


10
92,378
369,512
2.2 TB



(K=9, n=2, using complex128 = 16 bytes per entry)
At depth 6, the dense matrix already exceeds the RAM of a typical workstation. At depth 8, Optimal 2 handled it in 0.088 seconds across 4 ranks using only 509,078 nonzeros. At depth 10, distributed sparse assembly is the only feasible approach — the dense matrix would be 2.2 TB.

title: Week 4: Building the HEOM RHS from Scratch and Going Fully Distributed
text:
Key Insights from This Week
1. The coupling-up term does not involve bath coefficients. QuTiP’s _grad_next_bosonic always computes -i(Q\rho - \rho Q) regardless of exponent type. The bath coefficients c_k only appear in the coupling-down term. This is a consequence of the HEOM derivation: the upward coupling represents the action of the bath on the system, while the downward coupling represents feedback from the higher-tier ADOs back into the dynamics.
2. The RHS is block-sparse by construction, not by accident. Each ADO label \mathbf{n} connects only to \mathbf{n} \pm \mathbf{e}_k for each k. The number of nonzero blocks per row is bounded by 2K (two neighbours per mode), giving an average of roughly 2K \cdot n^2 nonzeros per ADO block row — independent of the total system size.
3. Diagonal initial states hide bugs. When \rho_0 is diagonal (like |0\rangle\langle 0|), the commutator [Q, \rho_0] vanishes for Q = \sigma_z, making the first timestep of the solution insensitive to errors in the coupling-up term. Always validate with a non-diagonal initial state (e.g. \rho_0 = |+\rangle\langle +|) to catch this class of bug.
4. PETSc’s ownership range is the natural parallelism primitive. A.getOwnershipRange() returns (rstart, rend) — the global row indices this rank is responsible for. Building only those rows locally and calling A.setValue() is all that is needed. PETSc handles the communication transparently during assembly and matrix-vector products.
5. Load balance depends on the ADO ordering. The current implementation assigns contiguous rows to each rank. Since lower tiers are denser, this leads to 2× load imbalance between early and late ranks. Tier-aware partitioning (interleaving rows from different tiers) would equalise this at the cost of non-contiguous ownership, which PETSc supports but requires more careful index management.

The depth-8 result (Optimal 2) is the headline number: a 97,240-dimensional sparse ODE solved in 88 milliseconds on 4 cores, using a matrix that would require 151 GB in dense form. The matrix was assembled in a distributed fashion from scratch, bypassing QuTiP entirely.

title: Week 4: Building the HEOM RHS from Scratch and Going Fully Distributed
text:
Next Steps
The immediate priorities for next week are:

Fix the coupling-up bug in Optimal 1 — use -i \cdot \text{comm}_Q instead of -i(c_k Q_l - c_k^* Q_r), and validate with a non-diagonal initial state.
Implement tier-aware partitioning — assign rows from each tier to ranks in a round-robin fashion to equalise the nonzero-per-rank distribution.
Scaling benchmarks — run Optimal 2 at depths 4, 6, 8, 10 and plot runtime vs depth and runtime vs number of MPI ranks to quantify strong and weak scaling.
Monitor function for trajectory — the current PETSc TS setup only returns the final state. Adding a monitor function will allow recording \langle\sigma_z\rangle(t) at each timestep for full trajectory validation.


Source files: heom_optimal1.py, heom_optimal2.py*
